sentences.text: Well, they usually had 1,000 people.
And we had to gather in a big hall.
It was a fairground, you know, like the, the fairs would be taking place in that building.
Today, it's not there anymore.
There's a big hotel.
But at that time, we all gathered.
Some people had beds.
But I was 992, so there were no beds for, for people after probably 900 or so.
So we were lying on the floor.
And we stayed there, I think, a day and a half or something.
I know for sure one night then, because they had to do all the registration and everything.
My brother and my mother went before me.
They went in August.
You see, it went by addresses.
It didn't mean that if you were one family that they put you all together.
If you lived in one place and your mother lived in another place and your brother, it always went by the address.
So they lived somewhere else, and I lived somewhere else.
So they went in August, and I went in September.
They put us on a train.
I don't know how many bags it had, or how many cars, rather, it had.
They were cattle cars.
And they put us in there.
And we went from Prague to [?
Boleslawiec. ?] At that time, the train did not go directly into Terezin.
It only went till [?
Boleslawiec. ?] And there, they, they threw us out from the train.
And we had to take whatever we had and march till Terezin.
It was quite a, a march.
Everybody had one suitcase or one huge bag.
And you know, we, we took whatever we thought would be good for us.
We also took some groceries, like sugar, and cream of the wheat, and dry goods.
And then we arrived.
Again, they put us into a, an armory, and where they went through our things.
And they would tell you where you have to go, to which armory.
They, they divided the people-- the younger ones, the older ones, the sick ones, and so on.
So I ended up, it was called the [INAUDIBLE] Armory.
There were, there were sick people and older people.
I worked there as a helper in a, a hospital.
I was like a junior nurse, I would say.
And I would wash the patients.
And in the morning, I would come in and-- mostly older men.
And they would call me Sunshine, because I always had a smile on my face.
And so I made their day.
And my mother worked as a nurse.
I met them after.
I don't remember how long I didn't see them-- a few weeks probably.
And then I met them.
My, my mother was a nurse.
And she nursed at-- there was scarlet fever.
So she was a nurse in scarlet fever.
And my brother, he made wooden toys.
They had like a little factory going.
And that's what he was doing.
We didn't see each other very often.
Probably once a week, I would go there in the evening and just chat with my mother.
And, and transports were coming every week, at first only from Bohemia, Moravia, but then they started coming from Germany, and from Holland, and from Denmark, and from Austria.
And it was the Tower of Babel.
It was a very surrealistic place, to me at least, the way I looked at it.
Because you see so many scenes which were out of the ordinary.
For instance, bread was delivered on a hearse, the old fashioned hearse, like used to be in Europe.
They were drawn by horses, of course.
So instead of the horses, there were two men.
And the bread was stacked up in the hearse.
So you saw that on the, on the street.
You saw girls going to work in the gardens.
You saw people cleaning the street.
Everybody did something.
We all worked, no matter what.
But everybody was very busy.
They had two homes for young boys.
And they had, they had homes for sick people.
We had mentally ill.
We had scarlet fever, typhoid, and-- well, I don't know you say that in English-- encephalitis.
That's a brain-- I think the cover of the brain is an infection there.
And, and then we had police, Jewish police.
And they took young, handsome men.
And they dressed them up in a uniform.
It was like in an operetta-- you know, the high hats with epaulettes.
And they didn't have much of a power, but they would be there when we would line up for our food.
So people would be, you know, in line, or that if somebody started-- maybe if they have a little fight or something.
So they were there.
We did also have gendarmes, Czech police.
And then we had the SS who really weren't-- it only-- they only were in, at headquarters.
We had a government.
There was the elder of the Jews.
And then you had the finance department, and the agriculture department, and the school department.
I mean, it really was like a city or a state on itself.
At that time, his name was Edelstein-- Jacob Edelstein.
Well, it depended how-- in the armories, they had larger rooms, and they had small rooms.
In the room-- I was in the different rooms.
You know, you sometimes, you were in one room.
Then they moved you in another room.
Sometimes we were eight people.
Sometimes we were 10 people.
And sometimes you had your own bunk.
Sometimes the banks were-- like in the big armories, they had huge rooms.
It's like the soldiers were in these huge rooms.
And they were stacked by three, you know, three beds.
And people when-- if they were not-- if there wouldn't have been the fear of transports going away, which started to take place, it wouldn't have been so terrible.
We could have managed.
But there was always this fear that-- of the unknown.
Where are they going to take us?
What is going to happen to us?
After all, it was 1943 by that time.
And we didn't know how the war was going.
We didn't know what was going, nothing.
We just lived in this ghetto.
And everybody was worried to have enough food, to have some clothing, and just to exist.
What did you eat each day?
In the morning, we would get some brown liquid.
And at-- then we would get, at noon, maybe a dumpling with some brown sauce.
We used to call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And it was like brown and had little yellow dots in it that was from the margarine or whatever.
And we had to line up.
And they would give it to you.
And then we would go and eat it.
We would have potatoes, and rutabaga, turnip.
I cannot eat it.
We had it all the time.
It smells terrible.
But that's what we lived on.
Probably we were, but, but that wasn't-- we don't even-- you know, when you look back on it, that isn't the, the most important part what you think of, that you were hungry.
As I said, we, we took a little bit of like cream of wheat or things like that with us.
So we kind of started to substitute.
And there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a barter trade going on.
One had this, and the other one had that.
So you started to barter.
As a matter of fact, I, I bought-- I don't remember for what-- but I bought an old suit, a man's suit.
And I found someone who would make a-- for me a suit.
We were very fashion-conscious.
We wanted to look nice.
We wanted to appear that we are young girls.
So I found this, this woman who was a seamstress.
And she made a very nice suit from me.
And I found somewhere, I don't know, a piece of fur.
And I would have a fur collar.
I had a hat, you know like a Cossack hat.
So it was very fashionable.
We also bartered for blankets, like tartan blankets.
And all the young girls would wear tartan skirts.
It, it was the most amazing thing to be in a place like that and still wanting to look nice.
We did.
We wanted to be young women.
We, you know, we, we had boyfriends.
We fell in love.
We made love.
After all, we were all in our teens.
So it was quite normal.
And I think it was good that we wanted to be normal, especially in Terezin.
People-- unfortunately, some had children, which wasn't so good.
And people lived together.
They were in the roof, under the roof.
They made, it was called, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And so they, they furnished it.
They found all kinds of furniture.
They, they made like little homes.
It, it was most incredible thing.
I don't think that anybody really talks about it, but I like to talk about the way we lived.
I didn't live like that.
But I did go visit people like that.
And we had, you know, we had like get-togethers.
And it was nice.
Of course, there was also a lot of culture in Terezin.
After all, the best musicians and writers and doctors from all over Europe were brought to Terezin.
So we did have concerts.
We had operas.
We had a children's opera, which is now being performed in North America.
It's called And really was Hitler.
We made fun of him.
But, thank god, the Germans didn't know.
They-- we-- was performed, which is, of course, the most famous Czech opera by Smetana, and And so we did go in the evening.
And the Germans kind of let us do that.
I don't know why, but somehow, Hitler wanted to keep us quiet.
And that was a very good part of it.
They-- later on, they even had stores for us.
What did they sell?
They sold the clothes they took from us.
And then we could-- we got money, ghetto money.
And we could go in the stores and, and buy things, which was ridiculous.
Nobody went there very much.
There was nothing really much to buy.
It was in, I think, the fall of '43, they started to prepare for the Red Cross to come in and to see what a model ghetto it was.
So they-- we even had a cafe house with a orchestra.
It was ridiculous, the whole thing.
You know, I, as I say, it was a surrealistic place to live in.
It-- nothing was real.
It was all just make believe.
Even we were make believe.
Did you have a feeling of unreality then?
Yes, it was very much.
You just looked out, and you saw all these people walking.
And, and everybody was doing something ridiculous.
I mean, we did-- there were people working in, in, in the laundry and in the bakery.
And people were burying people.
Every morning you looked out into the-- you know, the, the, the armories were built like that, so you had a space, a courtyard.
And you looked out.
And you had 10 or 20 coffins there.
People who died during the night, they were all there.
And then they took them away.
So it was a most-- very strange place.
No, no, I wasn't there.
No, we didn't know that, no.
But you see, the, the terrible thing was that the transports to the east continued.
The-- people came.
And people left.
By that time, the train tracks already went in Terezin.
So they didn't have-- I mean, you know, they, they made it more efficient.
You know, Germans, always efficient.
So they brought the, the train tracks right into, into Terezin.
So they-- the people didn't have to walk like we did.
And so when we left, we also left.
So nobody really saw us very much.
Because they did it all inside of the camp.
And my mother was called up to go wherever it was.
And I was very distraught, because everybody thought, you know, when we leave, it's not going to be very good.
And only she was called-- not my brother, not myself.
And my brother came to me.
And he said that he is going on his free will with her.
And I said that I think it's, it's great.
But I don't think it's a good idea.
Because, after all, where my mother is going, he cannot help her.
And today, we have to try to survive, whoever can.
And I know he was very attached to her.
But to go with her, if he could help her, that would be one thing.
But I didn't think that he could.
But he did go with her.
So they left.
Oh, but I forgot.
Before that, my mother once also was called to the transport.
And somehow, because she was a nurse, you know, in typhoid, they took her out.
So I already cried that she left.
And I came back to my room.
And there she was, sitting.
So it was kind of she was reborn again.
But, unfortunately, it didn't last too long.
So they left Terezin on the 15th of September, '43.
And I followed on the 15th of-- they went in September.
And I followed on the 15th of December, '43.
Before you left, were you were punished for anything while you were at Terezin?
No.
Yes, you saw people being struck.
Let's say they, they were walking.
And, and they thought they were not walking where they wanted or did something or, you know, there were incidents.
But on the whole, it wasn't that much.
In Terezin, no.
Well, we had the star from, from Prague.
After all, we used to wear the star already in Prague.
Yes.
Mhm.
That was a big problem.
But, fortunately, later on, we lost our, our periods.
It, it was good that we didn't have to worry about that.
But, on the other hand, now we are paying for it.
Because people who-- women who lose their menses suffer from osteoporosis.
It is just like the distance runners.
You know, they also lose their menses.
And they also, later on, suffer from osteoporosis.
There was a, there's a man in, in-- a doctor in Florida.
He did a study on survivors.
And he found out that many survivors now suffer from osteoporosis, but mostly women because we lost our menses.
I suppose there were.
I'm, I'm not privy to that.
But I'm quite sure.
So, yes, I was called to the transport on the 15th of December, '43.
And I, I had a very bad sore throat.
I probably had a 103 or 104 temperature, but that didn't deter anybody to take me out.
And I had a young friend by the name of Hanus [?
Gerst ?] who came to see me.
And he would say, don't worry.
My mother is going to send you parcels wherever you are.
She, she, she was the most remarkable woman.
She used to come to Terezin.
She was Jewish.
She became Jewish when she married Mr.
Gerstl.
And her son was taken.
And she was just broken.
But she would be able to have some-- she would pay the gendarmes.
And she would be able to walk-- we would see her walking.
And she would bring stuff into for him.
So he said, don't worry.
She's going to send you parcels.
That was the night before I left.
And then they put us into cattle cars.
And we were going-- we did not know where we were going.
And then the next day, we were overnight, [INAUDIBLE].
And the next day, we arrived in a place.
We didn't know what it was.
They opened the trucks-- the, the cars.
And they yelled, raus, raus!
And they had dogs there.
And there were trucks there And we saw some people in striped uniforms.
Mostly spoke-- they spoke mostly Polish.
And they would say to us, don't go on the trucks.
If you go in the trucks, you, you, you going to, you going to go to God.
But you, you had to go in the trucks.
There was no way out, you know?
We didn't know what they meant.
But, you know, the SS were standing there with their dogs.
So you had to go on the trucks.
So they took us into a camp, and all women separate, and all men separate.
And so we were sitting there for a while.
And then they started taking us to, to give, to tattoo our-- us.
And I had on me that suit the lady made.
And it was quite tight.
And I couldn't move my. my, my sleeve very high up.
So my tattoo is quite low, whereas others have it up.
And once we were tattooed, then they took us out.
And we went to the showers.
70-6-6-3-- See, I had to think.
I didn't remember anymore.
It was in, it was in Birkenau.
It was at Camp B2B.
And so they took us to the showers.
That was really the place where the gas was.
But we were fortunate.
We got the showers.
And so they took all our clothes away.
And they put us-- they put some stuff on-- I'm continuously saying stuff-- some green liquid on our hair to get rid of the lice, which we didn't have.
And the only thing we were allowed to keep were our stockings.
There was a bench near the wall.
And there, we put our stockings.
So I took the shower.
And, of course, there were no towels or anything.
And I went looking for my stockings.
And they were gone.
And here it was December in Poland, like December in Canada.
I was frantic, not having anything.
So there was another pair of stockings, so I took those.
And then they took us in another room.
And they threw some clothes at us.
And, you know, it was horrible.
The tall ones got little clothes.
And the little one had long clothes.
There was one girl.
I think her name was [?
Gregor. ?] I'm not quite sure.
But her-- she held reddish hair.
And her hair turned completely green.
And you know, we looked like scarecrows.
We were the only women in the whole of Birkenau and Auschwitz who had hair.
They didn't shave our heads.
All the other prisoners heads were shaved.
Why they didn't shave our hair, we don't know.
So everybody was very jealous of us.
So when we spend the night there, and the next day they brought us back to the-- to B2B.
And I don't exactly remember the name-- the number of the, of the house, or the block, we used to call it.
And there were these bunks, three stories again.
And of course, because we were young, the young ones went on top.
And the older ones were in the bottom.
The bottom was terrible.
It was just like-- it was always night there.
We were not allowed to go out for quite some time.
We only were allowed to go out to the toilets.
We had to line up.
And then, well, let's say, 10:20, they would let us out.
And it was a block.
The toilets were cement blocks with holes in it.
And that's where we went, and also to get washed.
Now, to get washed was a very important part.
To keep your hygiene really is extremely important in such circumstances, because it keeps you human.
If you have dignity, you have to keep your-- you have to be clean.
If you let yourself go, which very many young men did, and they looked terrible, and you lose all your dignity.
You really become, as we call them, a Muselmann.
You know, they were walking around like skeletons.
Their, their coats were long.
They, they used to wear hats.
But they cut, they cut the, the-- how do you call the-- the rim in the back.
And they had just a front.
They looked terrible.
The young men just looked awful, whereas we tried to keep ourselves pretty much in shape, even with the horrible clothes we had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We tried to be decent, kind of.
We wanted to have bras, because that was something a civilized woman would wear.
So, for instance, I go and speak to young people a lot.
And I tell them, you want to be free, and you do not want to wear bras.
We wanted to be dignified, and so we wanted to have bras.
We found some old pieces of material.
Somebody had a needle, which was worth probably $100,000 and-- in our eyes.
And we started sewing bras.
It, it was very important for us to, to stay human.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found my mother after, I think it was three weeks.
She was in a terrible state.
After all, she was not that old.
She was at that time in her early-- she must have been 52.
She looked just terrible.
She was down in the bottom bed.
And you know, there's no daylight.
It was awful.
So I, I felt I had to do something to help.
They, they had three girls always to, to bring the soup.
And the soup, which you ate at lunchtime, which wasn't very much of a soup, but it was water.
And it was brought in huge barrels.
And the barrels were carried by girls.
There were wooden poles.
And you would have-- you know, and you would walk like-- kind of like this.
The thing was that when you, when, when you went to pick up the empty barrels, you could scrape out what was left there.
Not very much, but there was something, and that you could keep.
And so I thought if I could get this extra soup, I could help her.
So I volunteered to, to carry those barrels.
And it was very hard.
It was such hard work.
So I did, and I brought her the soup.
But unfortunately, she couldn't eat anymore.
She was so very ill.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was end of December.
So I only was there two weeks.
And the next time I came-- of course, you see, you could not just walk-- like I was in the lower block.
I think I was number 11, and she was number 27.
So it was quite a walk.
And you were not allowed just to walk there.
You had to have permission.
And if the elders of the camp saw you and asked what were you doing there, he, you know, he could punish you.
He-- I went once walking.
And he said, where are you going?
I says, I'm going to Block 27 and see my mother.
I got one.
But that didn't really matter.
And I came there.
And they already moved her to the last bed, the last, you know, these bunks, without-- we had not very much to lie on.
But we did have a, a thing out of-- made out of straw.
But then they were so sick and had so much diarrhea, they, they just were lying on the boards.
So I found my mother there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was nearly dead.
But, unfortunately, she had a very strong heart, and she couldn't die.
But she died on January 4th, '40-- '43-- wait a minute, '44.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I went down where my brother was.
And I asked for him, because there was always a guard.
One of the prisoners would be standing at the door.
And I asked for him.
And I-- he came.
And I told him.
He didn't say anything.
He just turned around.
And, and he went away.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What we did in Birkenau [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was not very much.
We didn't work hardly at all.
We-- most of the time, we spend on our bunks.
We were cooking.
OK.
